speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you compensate that I'm louder than normal?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think it's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't speak louder, because too loud not good for me. The way you are is fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you pronounce the name of your city for me? 'Vladimitch.' Say it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not spell it. Say it just so that I can hear the sp--

speaker: interviewee
text: The way it was said in Poland before the war, 'Vlogemish Wolynski.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. But 'Vladimitch?'

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Vlogemish.'

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Vlogemish.'

speaker: interviewee
text: That's as Polish word.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, because I might want to use the word, and I wanted to get it right.

speaker: interviewee
text: A knight, the name of Wlodzimierz in Poland is--

speaker: interviewer
text: September 19, 1995. Survivor is Abram Geller, G-E-L-L-E-R. Interviewer is Ruth Resnikoff, R-E-S-N-I-K-O-F-F. Pembroke Pines, Florida. Interview is in English. This will be Tape One. Today is September 19, 1995, and I would like to introduce survivor Mr. Abram Geller, G-E-L-L-E-R. And I am the interview, Ruth Resnikoff, R-E-S-N-I-K-O-F-F. And we're in their apartment in Pembroke Pines, Florida. And now we will begin. Tape One. Will you tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Abram Geller.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name when you were in Poland or did you have a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, my parents used to call me Avrum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on October 15, 1990-- 1919, in Wlodzimierz Wolynski. That was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the town-- the total population was about between 25-- and 30,000 people. And the majority, I would say 75%, was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I remember my grandparents very well. My grandfather died in-- in 1935. And my grandmother was still alive when I left Wlodzimierz in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they live with-- did this grandmother live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We lived-- all-- all our family lived in a three-family house owned by us. And one apartment was where we lived, me and my father, my mother, and brother and sister. The other apartment was occupied by my grandfather and grandmother. And they had a daughter, Sonia. That was my aunt. And the third apartment lived my uncle and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: This apartment, did it have running water, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The apartment didn't have no running water. Water had to be brought from the-- what do you call it? Outside, like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A pump?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a pump. It was like an opening, and we had to pull out the water with a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A well?

speaker: interviewee
text: A well, yeah-- a well, yeah. And you had to pull out the water with the pails and bring it to the house. The water has to be brought into the house. There was no running water in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had electricity, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting that you had the one and not the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a serious man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Serious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a-- he was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the lumber business. He used to buy forests, pieces of forest, and he used to cut down the trees. And then he had a sawmill, and he used to manufacture lumber. He owned a sawmill, and it was out of town. And I remember he used to leave every Monday morning to that place where we had our sawmill and come back for Shabbat on Friday afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went twice with him, but I was 13, 14 years old, during the vacation time. And he had his house, a-- a wooden house near the sawmill. And he spent most of his time and his life in that-- in that sawmill. He used to come only for the weekend. So I hardly saw my father except for the holidays and weekends.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he came for Shabbat, did he daven? Was he religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. It was Shabbat. He observed Shabbat and all the holidays, you know. But he wasn't like I mean, Orthodox, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we spoke Yiddish with my parents. In school I went, and spoke the only language was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Can you describe any of the Jewish holidays, any of the customs that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing that I remember was like about every Saturday, I used to go to shul with my father. And in our town, was two big concentrations of military. I mean, it used to be their steady headquarters-- military headquarters. And naturally, Poland was quite a large country. There always was Jewish soldiers. And they used to come to shul. And my father, every Saturday, used to bring a soldier home for dinner. And that's what I remember. It was always-- Saturday's dinner was always somebody else from around Poland. Some people were from Western Poland, you know. Maybe our part was Eastern Poland. And we always had a soldier in our house for any holiday and Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat-- What did you eat on those Shabbat dinners or holiday dinners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess we started, told me, with chicken soup. And chicken, I mean, everything, I mean. But that was usually our meat meal, because that was the Shabbat meal with challah, with everything, a Jewish meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your schooling, tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My schooling, my father always believed in schooling. And for Jewish people and Poland, it was very hard to get into a public school, so he sent me to a private school. I finished the private school. It's like a regular elementary school. And then I attended a gymnasium. There was a private gymnasium, because there was always a-- there was also a public gymnasium in our town with no charge. But no Jews could have got into that school. The school had maybe 500 students, and I think they kept one or two Jewish students to show that there's no discrimination.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father sent me to a private gymnasium when I was 13, until I finished at school when I was 18. Now, I also attended, in my younger years, when I was-- before I started public school, Hebrew preschool. They call it Tarbut, what I attended. Did you notice in your early-- in your schooling as a young boy anti-Semitism in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There was no question about it. The first thing that you couldn't get into that-- that public gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were just like better one, better people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean, personally, did they throw stones at you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, we had-- our school had only Jewish-- was only Jewish. So I wasn't in contact with any-- any Polacks. The only time that, you know, that you had to-- we always were aware they were Jewish, you know. Like at nighttime, we walked at nighttime, but in the late '30s already you had to think about it. It became dangerous to walk at nighttime. And when it comes Catholic holidays like Christmas, we all knew that we had to stay home, not to go out at night, because plenty occasions, whenever they found a Jew, they beat him up. So there was a rule ready, and everybody knew that on the Polish holiday, especially Christmas and Easter, nobody went out from the house. We stayed home. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone in your family ever get beaten up? Did anyone in your family ever get beaten up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we were fortunate in that aspect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really were not poor. You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- I, never. We were never poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father made a good living. And he was considered a wealthy man in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for pleasures?

speaker: interviewee
text: For pleasure? Well, I used to-- I like used to-- I used to like to go-- we had a river in our town. I used to like to go swimming. I used have a-- I used to rent a kayak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go-- it wasn't a big river, but quite-- we used to go for with all the-- with the kayaks on the river. And in the wintertime, I tried skiing, but I wasn't very good at it, at the  skiing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also told me you played sports in school. Sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sports? I played soccer when I was 12, 13, up to 15, 16 years. I used to like soccer. I used to go to see those soccer games we had in town. That was Sunday. I used to enjoy them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you told me that you were a member of some clubs in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in gymnasium, we had like certain groups. And I always liked the history, and I was interested in any kind of travel. And we had a history club, and I was elected president of that history club. And we had meetings once a month, and we discussed different history matters and situations-- world situations-- and also planned excursions, like our school every year had the excursion. We went-- the first excursion, we went-- we went to Krakow. And from there, we went to Wieliczka. That's in Wieliczka, they have a salt mine what's famous all over the world. It's on the ground with all different figures, and it was very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every year, we went someplace else. And my father-- it was quite expensive, but my father never said no to any excursion we had to make. He was always for it. And he told me, any group-- you go with a group-- don't worry about it. I'll pay the bill. [LAUGHS] During this time, in the 1930s, Hitler came to power. Did you hear about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's-- of course, I heard about Hitler, but I was young, and I really didn't realize what cloud came over Europe that would have started. Well, as a boy in school, I didn't feel directly any-- any difference, you know, because in Poland, it started already-- the-- the government started to-- they used to say, economic we are-- we don't want to kill any Jews or any-- but-- but we want economic pressure is allowed, that's like-- I mean, against the Jews. So it started to be felt. But as a boy in school, I didn't feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did this economic pressure mean to your everyday life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Means-- it says in Polish, [POLISH]. In other words, you can fight on the-- on the commerce, like against the Jews, you know. When it comes to do business, to discriminate against Jews, but not to deprive them of their life, you know. It was following the German style when the Germans in the late '30s started to already to take away the businesses from Jews in Germany and expel them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this-- but did this economic war, did it affect your father's lumber business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't affect my father, because my father was a small businessman, you know. Most of the stuff what he-- when he-- in the sawmill, we had our lumber yard in our town, and it was sold retail. On certain years, I remember he used to ship finished wood to-- to Danzig-- that's a Polish port-- and it went to England, that-- that wood-- that lumber. But mostly we did business in-- in retail, in our lumber yard that was now where we lived, right behind our town-- our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think would have been your life work? What did you plan if there had been no war and life had been normal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father had some plans for me. When I finished high-- high school in '38, he wanted-- he realized that something is coming, you know. He had a better foresight than me. I was young then. I wasn't thinking about those matters. And he wanted me to have some kind of a profession. And-- and he sent me in 1938 to Vilna, to the Jewish Technicum, a technical school that was like an engineering school. It was a three-year school. And after finishing-- that was a well-known school in Poland. There was only one school like that, one Technicum. They call it the Vilna Technicum. After we finish the school, you used to be a technician in different departments. But you was easier to get. It was like a profession. But unfortunately, I went to the school just one year. In '39 the war broke out, and I was just ready to go to school when the war broke out September the 1st. And I was stuck in town. And that was my schooling. That was my end of schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one matter before we get into the war years that we didn't discuss. You were the oldest? No, you were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me who your brothers and sisters were, with their names and--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] My brother was three years younger than me. He was-- his name was Pincus. And three years, in those years-- I don't know-- it was like a different-- it was a big difference. He had his friends. I had my friends. So we'd just been getting along. My sister was 10 years younger than me, my beautiful sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always had a feeling for her. She was my favorite.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bella.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was just 10 when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She finished like anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you tell me about what happened to them, tell me what happened to you in 1937, '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, our part, our town where we belonged, was just almost a border town. Poland was divided between the Russians and the Germans. We were occupied by the Russian Army. And the life was already the same. My-- my father was considered a wealthy man by the Russians, and they took-- right away they took away the sawmill and left the house. We lived still in same house. And it was just-- my father was trying to get-- he used to be a bookkeeper during the First World War I, and he got a job as a bookkeeper for the city-- for the city of Wlodzimierz. And I didn't have anything what to do. And my father-- also we were on the list to-- to be-- to be taken away to Russia, because they'd been taking out, the Russian, all the people who were wealthier. They were taking them out and taking them to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you occupied by the Russians first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were occupied by the Russians, yeah, in 1939. So my father used his influence. He knew lot of people, and it cost us some money also. Tried to get not to go to Russia, because we didn't feel like going. We lived there. We didn't realize what's what, but we didn't want to break up the family and that. And somehow we succeeded, that we remained in-- remained in-- in Wlodzimierz, in our town. But after-- me, after a couple months that I-- and I didn't have anything what to do, I didn't have any experience in anything practical, so I-- I got-- I took a course in Lemberg. Lwow is the name of it, the Polish name, Lwow. And they-- I finished that course, a six-month course, that for the Russian. And they send me to work in a town-- it's actually a village-- very close to the new Russian-German border. And I worked there until the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I left in 1940 for-- for Lwow, for that course, that's the last time I saw my family. Because when the war broke out in '41, I was already-- I was together with the-- with the Russians, and we were ordered to leave the place. And the Germans were right on the border, and we supposed to go east to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was on the truck with other workers, office workers, going to Russia. But on my mind, I just didn't feel good, that I felt I'm going to-- here I am. I'm going to go to Russia, save myself, and I'm going to leave my family. And I just didn't feel right about it. I felt that I couldn't do it. And the first chance when the truck stopped in Brody, I made up my--

speaker: interviewer
text: It stopped where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brody, B-R-O-D-Y. It's a town in Galicia close to Zlotchov. I made up my mind that I am not going to go to Russia, that I'm going to get off the truck and try to get home to my hometown. But the closest town was Zlotchov. And I knew I have an uncle, an aunt living in Zlotchov. They were about 30 kilometers from the place the truck stopped to Zlotchov. And I decided to go to Zlotchov. I came-- I came to Zlotchov. I walked to Zlotchov, and I came there already when the German planes were bombing the town. But when I came to my-- to my uncle, we still-- the Germans weren't there yet. So I stayed by my uncle, and the next day, the German came into Zlotchov. And I didn't realize who the Germans were. The next day after they came, I-- in the afternoon, I walked out to the street seeing what's doing, and right away I was hauled away to the place where the Germans got all the Jews. It was in a jail, where they used to-- where they gathered all the Jews. And they were killing them. It was just a massacre. When I came to that place, I didn't realize what's-- what's-- what's what. When I came to that place, I saw already a big grave with all the hundreds of Jews laying in that grave. And they hauled me into that grave, too. I was in the grave also. And I realized--

speaker: interviewer
text: Without shooting, they just put you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those people-- those people were already in the grave. They were dead. They were shooting all around. All around with machine guns, they were shooting those people. And I-- when they pushed me into the grave, and I realized that I'm still alive, the first-- my reaction, I covered myself with two dead bodies and shooting continued. And somehow, I felt-- I felt that people around me, but I felt that I'm still alive. And at sundown, the shooting stopped. And somehow I felt that I'm moving, that I'm alive. And I pulled out from that grave, and everything was quiet. And I got out slowly. I was wounded by my foot, but I still could walk. And I came in at night to my aunt's house. And that was my first experience with the Germans, the first day when I came to Zlotchov.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening in your hometown with your father and brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any-- during the time when I was in Zlotchov, I don't know what happened there, because there was no communication, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me you knew what happened to your-- you told me what happened to your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, what I want to say is when I was in Zlotchov under the Germans, I didn't have any contact with my family. I had only one postcard from my mother after six months when we were in Zlotchov, and the Germans were there, that everything is fine, they're alive, and everything is all right. For me, it was no way I-- I could get to them, because it was prohibited for Jews to do any travel. It was just impossible. Wherever you are, you had to stay there. And we were just hoping for the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
